5 min read
Just now

--

Accessibility testing has improved significantly over the past decade. Modern teams have access to powerful automated tools that detect common issues, accessibility audits integrated into CI pipelines, and established manual testing practices using real assistive technologies.
Yet one problem remains difficult to solve:
How do teams know when a code change unintentionally changes what a screen reader user experiences?
A button can still exist. A page can still pass automated accessibility checks. The application can still appear visually correct. But a small change to HTML structure, ARIA attributes, labels, or component behavior can silently alter the information presented to users of assistive technology.
This is the problem screen reader regression testing aims to address.
What Is Screen Reader Regression Testing?
Regression testing is the practice of verifying that a software change has not accidentally broken existing functionality.
In traditional software development, teams write tests to ensure that a new release does not introduce unexpected behavior:

• A checkout flow still works.

• An API still returns expected responses.

• A component still renders correctly.
Accessibility needs the same mindset.
A screen reader regression occurs when a code change modifies the accessible experience in an unintended way.
Examples:

• A button loses its accessible name.

• A form field loses its associated label.

• A component’s role changes unexpectedly.

• A previously announced state such as “expanded” or “selected” disappears.

• A navigation element is removed from the accessibility tree.
These changes may not create visible UI defects, but they can significantly impact users who rely on assistive technology.
Why Existing Accessibility Testing Does Not Fully Cover This Problem
Modern accessibility tooling is extremely valuable. Automated scanners can identify many important issues:

• Missing alternative text

• Invalid ARIA usage

• Missing form labels

• Color contrast problems

• Keyboard accessibility issues
However, most accessibility tools are primarily focused on identifying violations against established rules.
Regression testing asks a different question:
“Did this change alter the accessible experience compared to what existed before?”
That distinction matters.
A component may still technically satisfy accessibility requirements while unintentionally changing how it is announced.
Consider a simple example:
Before:
<button aria-label=”Submit form”>
Submit
</button>
A screen reader may announce:
“Submit form, button”
and after a refactor:
<button>
Submit
</button>
The component may still pass many accessibility checks. It is still a button. It still has text content.
But the user experience changed:
“Submit, button”
The additional context provided by the accessible name disappeared.
The issue is not necessarily that the new implementation is invalid. The issue is that a meaningful accessibility behavior changed without being intentionally reviewed.
Why Manual Screen Reader Testing Alone Does Not Scale
Testing with real assistive technology remains essential.
Experienced accessibility testers provide something automation cannot fully replicate:

• Real user workflows

• Platform-specific behavior

• Browser and screen reader combinations

• Contextual understanding

• Human judgment
However, manual testing has natural limitations.
Large applications can contain thousands of components and frequent releases. It is unrealistic for a tester to manually verify every accessibility announcement after every code change.
This creates a gap:
Automated accessibility testing is fast but rule-focused. Manual testing is accurate but expensive.
Screen reader regression testing aims to provide an additional layer of confidence between the two.
Introducing Speakable: Accessibility Regression Testing at the Semantic Level
Speakable approaches screen reader regression testing by analyzing the accessibility tree rather than comparing raw HTML.
This distinction is important.
HTML can change significantly without affecting accessibility. Conversely, a small HTML change can create a major accessibility difference.
Speakable focuses on the information that assistive technologies consume:

• Roles

• Accessible names

• Descriptions

• Values

• States

• Focusability

• Visibility within the accessibility tree
Instead of asking:
“Did the HTML change?”
Speakable asks:
“Did the accessible experience change?”
Comparing Accessibility Changes Before They Reach Production
Developers can compare two versions of a page:
npx @reticular/speakable before.html — diff after.html -f json
The comparison identifies meaningful accessibility changes:

• Added or removed nodes

• Changed roles

• Name changes

• State changes

• Focusability changes
For example:
Before:
<label for=”email”>
Email address
</label>
<input id=”email” type=”email” required>
After:
<input id=”email” type=”email” required>
A visual review may not immediately reveal a problem.
However, Speakable detects that the input’s accessible name changed:
Before:
Email address, edit, required
After:
Edit, required
The field still exists. The application may still function.
But the context available to a screen reader user has changed.
Integrating Accessibility Regression Testing Into CI/CD
Modern development teams already rely on automated checks before merging code.
Speakable is designed to fit into that workflow.
Teams can:

• Generate accessibility snapshots

• Compare changes between versions

• Detect meaningful regressions

• Fail builds when important accessibility information is removed
This enables accessibility review earlier in the development process.
Instead of discovering an issue after deployment, teams can identify potential regressions during development.
Modern development teams already rely on automated checks before merging code. Accessibility regression testing can follow the same workflow by comparing accessibility output as part of the development pipeline.
For example, Speakable can be integrated into a GitHub Actions workflow to analyze accessibility changes automatically during pull requests. This allows teams to detect meaningful changes to accessible names, roles, states, and other semantic properties before they reach production.
A complete example workflow is available here
Supporting Different Screen Reader Experiences
Screen readers do not all announce content identically.
Speakable models output for multiple major screen readers, including:

• NVDA

• JAWS

• VoiceOver

• Narrator
Different assistive technologies have different terminology and announcement patterns.
For example:

• VoiceOver may announce “dimmed”

• Narrator may announce “disabled”

• Different readers may present states in different orders
Understanding these differences is important when evaluating accessibility changes.
Where Speakable Fits (and Where It Does Not)
Speakable is designed to complement — not replace — manual accessibility testing.
Real screen readers remain the source of truth for final verification.
There are many areas where human testing remains necessary:

• Real user workflows

• Complex interactions

• Browser-specific behavior

• Screen reader settings

• Visual presentation issues

• Dynamic behavior requiring a real browser
Speakable focuses on a narrower problem:
Helping developers detect meaningful changes to the accessibility information their applications expose.
It provides an additional safety net for teams that want accessibility regressions to be reviewed as part of their normal engineering workflow.
The Future of Accessibility Regression Testing
As web applications become increasingly component-driven, accessibility behavior is becoming part of a component’s contract.
A button is not just a visual element.
A form field is not just an input.
A component’s accessibility semantics are part of the user experience.
Software teams already protect functionality through regression testing. Accessibility deserves the same level of engineering discipline.
Screen reader regression testing represents an emerging practice: continuously monitoring how applications communicate with assistive technologies and catching unintended changes before they reach users.
Speakable aims to make that process more accessible to developers by bringing accessibility regression testing closer to the workflows they already use. If your team is interested in exploring accessibility regression testing, you can try Speakable with the CLI, Node API, MCP integration, or the web tool.
